narrative_blocks.header: Killed in the car her husband was driving
narrative_blocks.body: In 1971 she died in an automobile crash near the couple's pre-Revolutionary house on Turkey Lane in Cold Spring Harbor, with Archibald at the wheel. They had married at Boston's Emmanuel Church in 1917.

bio.bio_blurb: Wife of Archibald Roosevelt, TR's son

narrative_blocks.header: Killed in the car her husband was driving
narrative_blocks.body: In 1971 she died in an automobile crash near the couple's pre-Revolutionary house on Turkey Lane in Cold Spring Harbor, with Archibald at the wheel. They had married at Boston's Emmanuel Church in 1917.

bio.bio_blurb: Wife of Archibald Roosevelt, TR's son

notable.notable_blurb: TR's son; wounded and decorated in two wars

narrative_blocks.header: The only man twice ruined by one wound
narrative_blocks.body: A grenade in New Guinea in 1943 shattered the same knee German fire had wrecked in France in 1918, leaving him the only American ever rated totally disabled for one wound in two wars. He came home with four Silver Stars.

narrative_blocks.header: The old lion is dead
narrative_blocks.body: When Theodore Roosevelt died in January 1919, Archibald wired the news to his scattered brothers and sister in five blunt words. He was still recovering from his French wounds at the time.

narrative_blocks.header: From Teapot Dome to a bond house
narrative_blocks.body: He resigned from Sinclair Oil during the Teapot Dome inquiry and, though never implicated, gave testimony damaging to his former employer. After the second war he founded the municipal-bond firm Roosevelt and Cross, which still trades today.

narrative_blocks.header: A hard turn to the far right
narrative_blocks.body: In his later years he joined the John Birch Society and founded the Veritas Foundation to root out what he called socialist influence at Harvard. In 1954 he published a pamphlet attacking the award of a Theodore Roosevelt medal to the diplomat Ralph Bunche.

narrative_blocks.header: The last of Edith's sons
narrative_blocks.body: He outlived every brother, the only son of Theodore and Edith to come through both World Wars alive. His stone at Oyster Bay reads, the old fighting man home from the wars.

bio.bio_blurb: TR's son; wounded and decorated in two wars

military_service.notes: Severely wounded in France and discharged with full disability; two Silver Star citations and the Croix de Guerre.
Commanded in New Guinea; wounded at the ridge named in his honor.

narrative_blocks.header: A Roosevelt who became a master Arabist
narrative_blocks.body: He spent his career as an intelligence officer and scholar of the Middle East, fluent in Arabic and steeped in the region's politics. His memoir traced a life lived largely in the shadows of the Cold War.

narrative_blocks.header: His son would steward the family name
narrative_blocks.body: His own son, born in 1942, grew up to chair the Theodore Roosevelt Association and carry the family's public legacy forward. The line of the president's soldier sons did not end on the battlefield.

cross_connections.display_label: a cousin and fellow CIA Middle East hand

bio.bio_blurb: TR's grandson; CIA officer and Arabist

narrative_blocks.header: She founded a press for Island writers
narrative_blocks.body: On Martha's Vineyard she started the Tashmoo Press and brought out two dozen books by and about Islanders, mentoring a generation of local writers. She had been an art editor at Viking Press, producing coffee-table picture books.

narrative_blocks.header: A free spirit in overalls and pearls
narrative_blocks.body: Outspoken and unconventional, she photographed Vineyard children and Wampanoag elders and founded an animal-spaying charity on the island. A first marriage to a Roosevelt grandson had not suited her taste for diplomatic life.

bio.bio_blurb: Vineyard publisher; Archibald Roosevelt Jr.'s first wife

notable.notable_blurb: TR's great-grandson; family historian and businessman

narrative_blocks.header: He won his great-grandfather a Medal of Honor
narrative_blocks.body: He led the long campaign that finally brought Theodore Roosevelt the Medal of Honor for San Juan Hill, awarded in 2001 more than a century late. It hangs now in the White House room that bears the family name.

narrative_blocks.header: He re-ran his great-grandfather's River of Doubt
narrative_blocks.body: On his fiftieth birthday in 1992 he rafted a thousand miles down the Brazilian river his great-grandfather had charted in 1914, finding that nothing and everything had changed. He has long carried the family's public memory as head of the Theodore Roosevelt Association.

narrative_blocks.header: From anti-war marcher to China investor
narrative_blocks.body: He marched on the Pentagon in 1967 and stood among the crowds at Woodstock before a career in finance and consulting. He came to chair a family investment firm spanning Boston and Shanghai.

cross_connections.display_label: first ran the River of Doubt with TR in 1914

bio.bio_blurb: TR's great-grandson; family historian and businessman

narrative_blocks.header: Nine dark novels, then silence
narrative_blocks.body: Between 1950 and 1962 she published nine novels of psychological menace, full of characters with hidden and dangerous second selves, then stopped writing for good. Patricia Highsmith, who almost never reviewed anyone, praised her first book.

narrative_blocks.header: An early voice of lesbian pulp fiction
narrative_blocks.body: Her frank treatment of gay and lesbian lives made her one of the daring postwar writers who opened American fiction to those themes. Long forgotten, her books were rediscovered and reissued in the early two-thousands.

narrative_blocks.header: A debutante who danced in South America
narrative_blocks.body: She came out in New York society in 1937, then made her living as a professional dancer in South America and Canada. She gave it up in 1945 to marry an artist and move to Paris.

narrative_blocks.header: She played down the Roosevelt name
narrative_blocks.body: She wrote under her first husband's surname and downplayed her famous lineage, wanting the work judged on its own merits. She married three times and spent her last years alone in the North Carolina woods with cats and chickens.

narrative_blocks.header: He dazzled French Vogue with citrus and ink
narrative_blocks.body: For a few brilliant years around 1950 his drawings lit up French Vogue, all citrus yellow and shocking pink under a draughtsman's easy line. He designed for ballet, theater, and films, including a Judy Garland musical.

narrative_blocks.header: He drew the covers of his wife's novels
narrative_blocks.body: Even after their tempestuous marriage ended he illustrated the jackets of Theodora's books, and she called him her estranged but still beloved Tom. His career, crowded with talent, never quite cohered before his early death.

bio.bio_blurb: Vogue illustrator; Theodora Keogh's first husband

narrative_blocks.header: The 'tugboat captain' who was a ferry steward
narrative_blocks.body: Drawn to him through her love of tugboats, she married him during her Hotel Chelsea years in New York, where she kept a small wildcat for company. He was in fact a steward on the Circle Line, not the tugboat captain of legend.

narrative_blocks.header: Her last husband, a North Carolina industrialist
narrative_blocks.body: She befriended his wife in the Carolina mountains, and after both their marriages dissolved she and Arthur wed in 1979. His death ten years later left her alone in the woods for her final years.

narrative_blocks.header: The quiet sister among soldiers and writers
narrative_blocks.body: Born between her decorated father's two wars, she lived more privately than her siblings and married into the Jackson family. She outlived all but the youngest of them.

cross_connections.display_label: was her father-in-law, the Nuremberg prosecutor
was her mother-in-law, who lived to ninety-five

narrative_blocks.header: He was his father's right hand at Nuremberg
narrative_blocks.body: Fresh from Harvard Law and the Navy, he served as personal assistant to his father, the chief American prosecutor of the Nazi leadership. That trial was the first case of his career.

narrative_blocks.header: A lifetime at one great firm
narrative_blocks.body: He spent his whole career at Milbank, Tweed, rising to chair the firm and head its litigation. His clients ranged from Nelson Rockefeller to Jacqueline Kennedy Onassis and the New York Stock Exchange.

narrative_blocks.header: He argued for a permanent war-crimes court
narrative_blocks.body: He edited the published record of the Nuremberg evidence and pressed in print for a standing international criminal court decades before one existed. He twice argued before the Supreme Court.

cross_connections.display_label: served the Rockefellers from Milbank Tweed too

narrative_blocks.header: Travel consultant and Roosevelt great-granddaughter
narrative_blocks.body: A graduate of Chapin and New York University, she worked as a travel consultant and was one of five Jackson daughters. She married Thomas White Hook in a ceremony followed by a reception at the River Club.

notable.notable_blurb: Literary agent to Pynchon and DeLillo

narrative_blocks.header: She represents the author no one can find
narrative_blocks.body: Melanie Jackson founded the Melanie Jackson Agency, representing a distinguished roster of authors including Nobel laureate Wole Soyinka, Pulitzer winners Ron Chernow and Steve Coll, and novelist Percival Everett. Her most famous client is the reclusive Thomas Pynchon.

narrative_blocks.header: Her most elusive client became her husband
narrative_blocks.body: Jackson began representing Thomas Pynchon in the 1980s, after the famously private novelist left his longtime agent. The two married in 1990 and have guarded their privacy ever since.

cross_connections.display_label: was her grandfather, the chief American prosecutor at Nuremberg

narrative_blocks.header: Her brief marriage to a Soviet defector
narrative_blocks.body: A Barnard graduate, she was married for four years to Alexander Barmine, a former Red Army general who had defected and become an American broadcaster. Their daughter Margot carried the Roosevelt name forward.

bio.bio_blurb: Daughter of Archibald Roosevelt; Barnard graduate
